content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


November 19, 1992


Timothy A. Hodson
Staff Director
Senate Committee on Elections
and Reapportionment
State Capitol, Room 5051
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-92-623

Dear Mr. Hodson:

     This is in response to your letter requesting assistance
regarding the mass mailing provision of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act"). Since your advice request does not refer to a
specific official government decision, we are treating your
request as one for informal assistance.¹ Please note that your
letter and our response are public records which may be disclosed
to the public upon receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

     You have asked about the application of the Milman Advice
Letter, No. I-91-567 (which was requested on behalf of the
Assembly Rules Committee) to State Senators. The Milman Advice
Letter concerns the scope of an exception to the prohibition of the
mass mailing Act.

     In the Milman Advice Letter we advised that persons residing
or doing business in an Assemblymember's district as it existed
prior to reapportionment continued to be the Assemblymember's
constituents for purposes of the exception in Regulation
18901(b)(9)(A)(1) until the next election. In addition, during

_______________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, commencing
at Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-92-623
Page 2


the interim period after reapportionment and before the November
elections, we advised that residents in geographic territory which
has been shifted to the district for which the Assemblymember
qualifies as an incumbent under Elections Code Section 10212 are
also the Assemblymember's constituents.

This conclusion, in part, was based on Friends of
Assemblywoman La Follette v. Superior Court (1982) 134 Cal.App.3d
832. The court stated:

     The construction we place on the statute means
     that each resident in California will continue to
     be serviced, until the voters make a choice in the
     1982 General Election, by the representative in the
     State Assembly who was selected at the 1980 General
     Election by voters in the district as it was
     defined. In addition, residents in geographic
     territory which has been shifted to a new and
     different assembly district may be serviced by the
     representative who qualifies as an incumbent under
     Elections Code section 10212. [Footnote omitted]

     You have not asked a question concerning any specific
Senator. As you may note from the Milman Advice Letter, in
reaching the conclusion with respect to the Assemblymembers, both
factual and policy issues were considered. Moreover, the analysis
in the Milman Advice Letter, by express terms, applies only to
the Assembly. (Milman Advice Letter, p. 1) Additionally, the
advice was applicable only until the November election (which has
now past). (Milman Advice Letter, p. 5.)

     You have provided no facts relating to reapportionment and
its effect on the various members of the Senate, or on their
constituents. Thus, while the Milman Advice Letter may be used
for general guidance under identical facts, you have provided no
facts to indicate that all Senators are similarly situated to the
Members of the Assembly.

     However, consistent with the Milman Advice Letter, persons
residing or doing business in a Senator's district as it existed
prior to reapportionment continues to be the Senator's constituent
for purposes of the exception in Regulation 18901(b)(9)(A)(1)
until the Senator is elected to his or her reapportioned district.
In addition, during the interim period after reapportionment and
before the reapportioned district, residents in
geographic territory which has been shifted to the district for
which the Senator qualifies as the incumbent under Elections Code
Section 10212 are also the Senator's constituents.


3    Commission advice is the application of the law to a particular
set of facts provided by the requestor. (See, Regulation
18329(b)(7).)

Our File No. I-92-623
Page 3


However, without a factual basis to your question, we will be
unable to provide assistance beyond this general statement of the
law.⁴ If you wish to submit facts pertaining to a specific
question, we can provide written advice applicable to the Senate
members.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.⁵

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW


4 For example, in my telephone conversation with Mr. Chesin, he
asked whether the newly elected Senators could continue to use the
"constituent meeting" exception to the mass mailing prohibition to
send mailings to persons in the Senators' old districts. We have
no facts to indicate that such mailings would fit the rationale in
the Mailman Advice Letter. If you have facts to indicate
otherwise, you may wish to submit them with a new advice request.

5 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.question:
,
we can provide written advice applicable to the Senate
members.

we can provide written advice applicable to the Senate
members.
